narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 48.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your third-year school decile ranking of 2—well below the typical student's 5.53—your LSAT score of 32 compared to the average 36.7, your first-year decile of 3 versus the usual 5.71, your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 against an average of 3.21, your high family income bracket, your status as a male applicant, your White racial background, and your plan to attend full-time. While most students in the dataset share similarities like being full-time (92.7%) or White (83.8%), your academic metrics stand out as notably lower than average, painting a picture of underperformance relative to peers who typically pass the bar. The strongest influence on this prediction came from your low third-year decile ranking, which significantly pushed the model toward expecting bar failure. Next in importance was your below-average LSAT score, which also nudged the prediction in the same concerning direction. Your first-year decile followed closely, reinforcing that early academic struggles mattered a lot here. Even your high family income played a role in tipping the scales toward failure, along with your undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status—all contributing in descending order of influence. In essence, no single factor saved the day; instead, your weaker academic indicators dominated the model's view compared to typical successful applicants. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control, like retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above the average, as it's a key lever and something many students improve with preparation. Building stronger academic habits now could help if you're pursuing further education, potentially lifting future grade deciles. Finally, dedicated bar exam prep courses have helped many in similar positions turn predictions around—consider starting early to build the skills the model flagged as risks. You've got potential; with targeted effort on these academics, a stronger application is within reach.
